revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.

{{公園 |名称 = 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園 |英語名称 = |画像 = [[ファイル:Haniwa-park 01.jpg|300px]] |画像キャプション = |国 = {{JPN}} |都市 = [[大阪府]][[高槻市]]上土室１丁目
{{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|51|14.1|N|135|35|4.6|E|region:JP}} |分類 = 史跡公園 |面積 = 4,163ｍ2 |前身 = |開園 = [[1995年]](平成7年)3月 |運営者 = 高槻市 |年来園者数 = |現況 = |設備・遊具 = |駐車場 = なし |バリアフリー = |建築家と技術者 = |告示 = |事務所 = |事務所所在地 = |アクセス = |備考 = |公式サイト = [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi/haniwae.html 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園] }} '''史跡新池ハニワ工場公園'''（しせきしんいけはにわこうえん）は[[大阪府]][[高槻市]]にある[[史跡]][[公園]]。 正式名称は「史跡今城塚古墳附新池埴輪製作遺跡」。 == 概要 == 史跡新池埴輪製作遺跡は、5～6世紀の遺跡で、埴輪窯18基と工房、工人集落からなる全国最大級の[[埴輪]]工場跡である。 昭和63年（1988年）から調査がはじまり、平成6年（1994年）ハニワ工場公園として整備された。 平成3年（1991年）7月20日、史跡[[今城塚古墳]]に追加指定された国指定の[[史跡]]。 [[今城塚古墳]]（高槻市郡家新町、6世紀前半）の埴輪はここで造られた。高槻市教育委員会文化財課「ハニワ工場公園」リーフレット == 施設 == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|ハニワ工場館（新池遺跡18号窯）]] * 工房2棟　3棟の内2棟を復元。 * 窯跡2基　大小あわせて18の窯の内、2基を復元。 * ハニワ工場館　新池遺跡最大級の18号窯（530年年頃）を覆屋で保護し、出土した埴輪3点も展示。 * プロムナード　20種類のハニワの複製品と漫画による説明パネル == 利用情報 == * 開館　10時～17時（公園内は終日オープン） * 休館　12月29日～1月3日 * 入館料　無料 == 周辺 == * 番山古墳 * [[今城塚古墳]] * [[高槻市立今城塚古代歴史館]] * [[太田茶臼山古墳]] == 交通アクセス == * [[JR京都線]][[摂津富田駅]]から高槻市営バス阿武山団地、日赤病院行、上土室下車東へ徒歩約5分。 == 出典 == {{DEFAULTSORT:しせきしんいけはにわこうえん}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:大阪府にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:大阪府の公園]] [[Category:高槻市]] [[Category:大阪府にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:大阪府の歴史]] [[Category:高槻市]]

{{公園 |名称 = 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園 |英語名称 = |画像 = [[ファイル:Haniwa-park 01.jpg|300px]] |画像キャプション = |国 = {{JPN}} |都市 = [[大阪府]][[高槻市]]上土室1丁目

{{公園 |名称 = 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園 |英語名称 = |画像 = [[ファイル:Haniwa-park 01.jpg|300px]] |画像キャプション = |国 = {{JPN}} |都市 = [[大阪府]][[高槻市]]上土室1丁目
{{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|51|14.1|N|135|35|4.6|E|region:JP-27}} |分類 = 史跡公園 |面積 = 4,163m2 |前身 = |開園 = [[1995年]](平成7年)3月 |運営者 = 高槻市 |年来園者数 = |現況 = |設備・遊具 = |駐車場 = なし |バリアフリー = |建築家と技術者 = |告示 = |事務所 = |事務所所在地 = |アクセス = |備考 = |公式サイト = [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園] }} {{osm box|w|156761606}} '''史跡新池ハニワ工場公園'''（しせきしんいけハニワこうばこうえん）は[[大阪府]][[高槻市]]にある[[史跡]][[公園]]。国指定の[[史跡]]。正式名称は「'''史跡今城塚古墳附新池埴輪製作遺跡'''」。 == 概要 == 史跡新池埴輪製作遺跡は、5 - 6世紀の遺跡で、埴輪窯18基と工房、工人集落からなる全国最大級の[[埴輪]]工場跡である。 『日本書記』欽明天皇23年条にある「摂津国三嶋郡植廬（ハニイホ）新羅人之先祖也」と考えられている。 昭和63年（[[1988年]]）から調査がはじまり、平成6年（1994年）ハニワ工場公園として整備された。 平成3年（[[1991年]]）7月20日、史跡[[今城塚古墳]]に追加指定された国指定の史跡である。 [[今城塚古墳]]（高槻市郡家新町、6世紀前半）の埴輪は当工場で造られた高槻市教育委員会文化財課「ハニワ工場公園」リーフレット。 == ハニワ工場の略年 == * 450年頃、太田茶臼山古墳の造営を目的として工場が開かれた。窯3基(A群)と工房3棟、住居7棟の規模。 * 480年頃、窯5基(B群)と住居7棟を追加。番山古墳などのハニワを生産。 * 530年頃、窯10基(C群)を追加。5棟の工房(住居兼用)。住居14棟。今城塚古墳向けのハニワを生産。 * 550年頃、ハニワ作りを終える。 == 施設 == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|ハニワ工場館（新池遺跡18号窯）]] * プロムナード ** 動物、円筒、家形、武人埴輪などの複製品を20種類、21体設置。本物と同寸法。磁器製。素焼きの質感を模しており自由に触れる。継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.157(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN4-642-07735-9) ** ヨシトミヤスオ氏の漫画による説明パネルを展示（陶板製）。 * 工房2棟 ** 3棟の内2棟を復元。竪穴式で母屋にあたる部分は茅葺き、周縁の下屋部分は杉皮葺きで復元されている。継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.158(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN4-642-07735-9) * ハニワ窯跡2基 ** 大小あわせて18の窯の内、2基を復元。また、1基を発掘中の状態で再現。 * ハニワ工場館 ** 新池遺跡最大級の18号窯（530年頃）を覆屋で保護し、出土した埴輪3点も展示。 == 利用情報 == * 開館時間：10時 - 17時（公園内は終日オープン） * 休館日：12月29日 - 1月3日 * 入館料：無料 == 周辺 == * 番山古墳 * [[今城塚古墳]] * [[今城塚古代歴史館]] * [[太田茶臼山古墳]] == 交通アクセス == * [[JR京都線]][[摂津富田駅]]から高槻市営バス阿武山団地、日赤病院行、上土室下車東へ徒歩約5分。 == 出典 == {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しせきしんいけはにわこうえん}} [[Category:大阪府の公園]] [[Category:大阪府にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:高槻市の歴史]] [[Category:高槻市の地理]]

『日本書記』欽明天皇23年条にある「摂津国三嶋郡植廬（ハニイホ）新羅人之先祖也」と考えられている。
[[今城塚古墳]]（高槻市郡家新町、6世紀前半）の埴輪は当工場で造られた高槻市教育委員会文化財課「ハニワ工場公園」リーフレット。 昭和63年（[[1988年]]）から調査がはじまり、平成6年（1994年）ハニワ工場公園として整備された。
平成3年（[[1991年]]）7月20日、史跡[[今城塚古墳]]に追加指定された国指定の史跡である。
== ハニワ工場の略年 == * 450年頃、太田茶臼山古墳の造営を目的として工場が開かれた。窯3基(A群)と工房3棟、住居7棟の規模。 * 480年頃、窯5基(B群)と住居7棟を追加。番山古墳などのハニワを生産。 * 530年頃、窯10基(C群)を追加。5棟の工房(住居兼用)。住居14棟。今城塚古墳向けのハニワを生産。 * 550年頃、ハニワ作りを終える。 == 施設 == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|ハニワ工場館（新池遺跡18号窯）]] * プロムナード ** 動物、円筒、家形、武人埴輪などの複製品を20種類、21体設置。本物と同寸法。磁器製。素焼きの質感を模しており自由に触れる。継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.157(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN4-642-07735-9) ** ヨシトミヤスオ氏の漫画による説明パネルを展示（陶板製）。 * 工房2棟 ** 3棟の内2棟を復元。竪穴式で母屋にあたる部分は茅葺き、周縁の下屋部分は杉皮葺きで復元されている。継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.158(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN4-642-07735-9) * ハニワ窯跡2基 ** 大小あわせて18の窯の内、2基を復元。また、1基を発掘中の状態で再現。 * ハニワ工場館 ** 新池遺跡最大級の18号窯（530年頃）を覆屋で保護し、出土した埴輪3点も展示。 == 利用情報 == * 開館時間：10時 - 17時（公園内は終日オープン） * 休館日：12月29日 - 1月3日 * 入館料：無料 == 周辺 == * 番山古墳 * [[今城塚古墳]] * [[今城塚古代歴史館]] * [[太田茶臼山古墳]] == 交通アクセス == * [[JR京都線]][[摂津富田駅]]から高槻市営バス阿武山団地、日赤病院行、上土室下車東へ徒歩約5分。 == 出典 == {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しせきしんいけはにわこうえん}} [[Category:大阪府の公園]] [[Category:大阪府にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:高槻市の歴史]] [[Category:高槻市の地理]]

{{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|51|14.1|N|135|35|4.6|E|region:JP-27}} |分類 = 史跡公園 |面積 = 4,163m2 |前身 = |開園 = [[1995年]](平成7年)3月 |運営者 = 高槻市 |年来園者数 = |現況 = |設備・遊具 = |駐車場 = なし |バリアフリー = |建築家と技術者 = |告示 = |事務所 = |事務所所在地 = |アクセス = |備考 = |公式サイト = [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園] }} {{osm box|w|156761606}} '''史跡新池ハニワ工場公園'''（しせきしんいけハニワこうばこうえん）は[[大阪府]][[高槻市]]にある[[史跡]][[公園]]。国指定の[[史跡]]。正式名称は「'''史跡今城塚古墳附新池埴輪製作遺跡'''」。 == 概要 == 史跡新池埴輪製作遺跡は、5 - 6世紀の遺跡で、埴輪窯18基と工房、工人集落からなる全国最大級の[[埴輪]]工場跡である。
『日本書記』欽明天皇23年条にある「摂津国三嶋郡植廬（ハニイホ）新羅人之先祖也」と考えられている。
[[今城塚古墳]]（高槻市郡家新町、6世紀前半）の埴輪は当工場で造られた高槻市教育委員会文化財課「ハニワ工場公園」リーフレット。 昭和63年（[[1988年]]）から調査がはじまり、平成6年（1994年）ハニワ工場公園として整備された。
平成3年（[[1991年]]）7月20日、史跡[[今城塚古墳]]に追加指定された国指定の史跡である。

{{公園 |名称 = 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園 |英語名称 = |画像 = [[File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-1.jpg|300px]] |画像キャプション = （手前から）1号・2号・3号埴輪窯
（1号・2号は復元） |国 = {{JPN}} |都市 = [[大阪府]][[高槻市]]上土室1丁目
{{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|51|14.1|N|135|35|4.6|E|region:JP-27}} |分類 = 史跡公園 |面積 = 4,163m2 |前身 = |開園 = [[1995年]](平成7年)3月 |運営者 = 高槻市 |年来園者数 = |現況 = |設備・遊具 = |駐車場 = なし |バリアフリー = |建築家と技術者 = |告示 = |事務所 = |事務所所在地 = |アクセス = |備考 = |公式サイト = [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園] }} '''史跡新池ハニワ工場公園'''（しせきしんいけハニワこうばこうえん）は、[[大阪府]][[高槻市]]にある[[史跡]][[公園]]。'''新池埴輪製作遺跡'''（国の[[史跡]]「今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡」の一部）を保存する公園である。 == 概要 == 新池埴輪製作遺跡は、5 - 6世紀の遺跡で、埴輪窯18基と工房、工人集落からなる全国最大級の[[埴輪]]工場跡である。一帯は『日本書記』欽明天皇23年条にある「摂津国三嶋郡植廬（ハニイホ）新羅人之先祖也」と考えられている。[[今城塚古墳]]（高槻市郡家新町、6世紀前半）の埴輪は当工場で造られた高槻市教育委員会文化財課「ハニワ工場公園」リーフレット。 遺跡域では[[1988年]]（[[昭和]]63年）から調査がはじまり、[[1994年]]（[[平成]]6年）に「ハニワ工場公園」として整備された。[[1991年]]（平成3年）7月20日、遺跡域が既指定の国の[[史跡]]「今城塚古墳」に追加指定され、史跡指定名称が「今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡」に変更。 == ハニワ工場の略年 == * 450年頃、太田茶臼山古墳の造営を目的として工場が開かれた。窯3基(A群)と工房3棟、住居7棟の規模。 * 480年頃、窯5基(B群)と住居7棟を追加。番山古墳などのハニワを生産。 * 530年頃、窯10基(C群)を追加。5棟の工房(住居兼用)。住居14棟。今城塚古墳向けのハニワを生産。 * 550年頃、ハニワ作りを終える。 == 施設 == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|ハニワ工場館（新池遺跡18号窯）]] * プロムナード ** 動物、円筒、家形、武人埴輪などの複製品を20種類、21体設置。本物と同寸法。磁器製。素焼きの質感を模しており自由に触れる。継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.157(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN 4-642-07735-9) ** ヨシトミヤスオの漫画による説明パネルを展示（陶板製）。 * 工房2棟 ** 3棟の内2棟を復元。竪穴式で母屋にあたる部分は茅葺き、周縁の下屋部分は杉皮葺きで復元されている。継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.158(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN 4-642-07735-9) * ハニワ窯跡2基 ** 大小あわせて18の窯の内、2基を復元。また、1基を発掘中の状態で再現。 * ハニワ工場館 ** 新池遺跡最大級の18号窯（530年頃）を覆屋で保護し、出土した埴輪3点も展示。 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|1号・2号工房（復元） File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|工房と埴輪窯跡（植え込み部分） ファイル:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|出土品
{{small|[[今城塚古代歴史館]]展示。}} == 利用情報 == * 開館時間：10時 - 17時（公園内は終日オープン） * 休館日：12月29日 - 1月3日 * 入館料：無料 == 交通アクセス == * [[JR京都線]][[摂津富田駅]]から高槻市営バス阿武山団地、日赤病院行、上土室下車東へ徒歩約5分。 == 周辺 == * 番山古墳 * [[今城塚古墳]] * [[今城塚古代歴史館]] * [[太田茶臼山古墳]] == 出典 == == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1805|今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html ハニワ工場公園] - 高槻市ホームページ「インターネット歴史館」 {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しせきしんいけはにわこうはこうえん}} [[Category:大阪府の公園]] [[Category:大阪府にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:高槻市の歴史]] [[Category:高槻市の地理]]

{{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|51|14.1|N|135|35|4.6|E|region:JP-27}} |分類 = 史跡公園 |面積 = 4,163m2 |前身 = |開園 = [[1995年]](平成7年)3月 |運営者 = 高槻市 |年来園者数 = |現況 = |設備・遊具 = |駐車場 = なし |バリアフリー = |建築家と技術者 = |告示 = |事務所 = |事務所所在地 = |アクセス = |備考 = |公式サイト = [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園] }} '''史跡新池ハニワ工場公園'''（しせきしんいけハニワこうばこうえん）は、[[大阪府]][[高槻市]]にある[[史跡]][[公園]]。'''新池埴輪製作遺跡'''（国の[[史跡]]「今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡」の一部）を保存する公園である。 == 概要 == 新池埴輪製作遺跡は、5 - 6世紀の遺跡で、埴輪窯18基と工房、工人集落からなる全国最大級の[[埴輪]]工場跡である。一帯は『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年条にある「摂津国三嶋郡植廬（ハニイホ）新羅人之先祖也」と考えられている。[[今城塚古墳]]（高槻市郡家新町、6世紀前半）の埴輪は当工場で造られた高槻市教育委員会文化財課「ハニワ工場公園」リーフレット。 遺跡域では[[1988年]]（[[昭和]]63年）から調査がはじまり、[[1994年]]（[[平成]]6年）に「ハニワ工場公園」として整備された。[[1991年]]（平成3年）7月20日、遺跡域が既指定の国の[[史跡]]「今城塚古墳」に追加指定され、史跡指定名称が「今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡」に変更。 == ハニワ工場の略年 == * 450年頃、太田茶臼山古墳の造営を目的として工場が開かれた。窯3基(A群)と工房3棟、住居7棟の規模。 * 480年頃、窯5基(B群)と住居7棟を追加。番山古墳などのハニワを生産。 * 530年頃、窯10基(C群)を追加。5棟の工房(住居兼用)。住居14棟。今城塚古墳向けのハニワを生産。 * 550年頃、ハニワ作りを終える。 == 施設 == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|ハニワ工場館（新池遺跡18号窯）]] * プロムナード ** 動物、円筒、家形、武人埴輪などの複製品を20種類、21体設置。本物と同寸法。磁器製。素焼きの質感を模しており自由に触れる。継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.157(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN 4-642-07735-9) ** ヨシトミヤスオの漫画による説明パネルを展示（陶板製）。 * 工房2棟 ** 3棟の内2棟を復元。竪穴式で母屋にあたる部分は茅葺き、周縁の下屋部分は杉皮葺きで復元されている。継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.158(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN 4-642-07735-9) * ハニワ窯跡2基 ** 大小あわせて18の窯の内、2基を復元。また、1基を発掘中の状態で再現。 * ハニワ工場館 ** 新池遺跡最大級の18号窯（530年頃）を覆屋で保護し、出土した埴輪3点も展示。 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|遠景 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|1号・2号工房（復元） File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|工房と埴輪窯跡（植え込み部分） ファイル:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|出土品
{{small|[[今城塚古代歴史館]]展示。}} == 利用情報 == * 開館時間：10時 - 17時（公園内は終日オープン） * 休館日：12月29日 - 1月3日 * 入館料：無料 == 交通アクセス == * [[JR京都線]][[摂津富田駅]]から高槻市営バス阿武山団地、日赤病院行、上土室下車東へ徒歩約5分。 == 周辺 == * 番山古墳 * [[今城塚古墳]] * [[今城塚古代歴史館]] * [[太田茶臼山古墳]] == 出典 == == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1805|今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html ハニワ工場公園] - 高槻市ホームページ「インターネット歴史館」 {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しせきしんいけはにわこうはこうえん}} [[Category:大阪府の公園]] [[Category:大阪府にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:高槻市の歴史]] [[Category:高槻市の地理]] [[Category:1995年開業の施設]]

{{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|51|14.1|N|135|35|4.6|E|region:JP-27}} |分類 = 史跡公園 |面積 = 4,163m2 |前身 = |開園 = [[1995年]](平成7年)3月 |運営者 = 高槻市 |年来園者数 = |現況 = |設備・遊具 = |駐車場 = なし |バリアフリー = |建築家と技術者 = |告示 = |事務所 = |事務所所在地 = |アクセス = |備考 = |公式サイト = [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園] }} '''史跡新池ハニワ工場公園'''（しせきしんいけハニワこうばこうえん）は、[[大阪府]][[高槻市]]にある[[史跡]][[公園]]。'''新池埴輪製作遺跡'''（国の[[史跡]]「今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡」の一部）を保存する公園である。 == 概要 == 新池埴輪製作遺跡は、5 - 6世紀の遺跡で、埴輪窯18基と工房、工人集落からなる全国最大級の[[埴輪]]工場跡である。一帯は『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年条にある「摂津国三嶋郡植廬（ハニイホ）新羅人之先祖也」と考えられている。[[今城塚古墳]]（高槻市郡家新町、6世紀前半）の埴輪は当工場で造られた高槻市教育委員会文化財課「ハニワ工場公園」リーフレット。 遺跡域では[[1988年]]（[[昭和]]63年）から調査がはじまり、[[1994年]]（[[平成]]6年）に「ハニワ工場公園」として整備された。[[1991年]]（平成3年）7月20日、遺跡域が既指定の国の[[史跡]]「今城塚古墳」に追加指定され、史跡指定名称が「今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡」に変更。 == ハニワ工場の略年 == * 450年頃、太田茶臼山古墳の造営を目的として工場が開かれた。窯3基(A群)と工房3棟、住居7棟の規模。 * 480年頃、窯5基(B群)と住居7棟を追加。番山古墳などのハニワを生産。 * 530年頃、窯10基(C群)を追加。5棟の工房(住居兼用)。住居14棟。今城塚古墳向けのハニワを生産。 * 550年頃、ハニワ作りを終える。 == 施設 == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|ハニワ工場館（新池遺跡18号窯）]] * プロムナード ** 動物、円筒、家形、武人埴輪などの複製品を20種類、21体設置。本物と同寸法。磁器製。素焼きの質感を模しており自由に触れる。継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.157(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN 4-642-07735-9) ** ヨシトミヤスオの漫画による説明パネルを展示（陶板製）。 * 工房2棟 ** 3棟の内2棟を復元。竪穴式で母屋にあたる部分は茅葺き、周縁の下屋部分は杉皮葺きで復元されている。継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.158(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN 4-642-07735-9) * ハニワ窯跡2基 ** 大小あわせて18の窯の内、2基を復元。また、1基を発掘中の状態で再現。 * ハニワ工場館 ** 新池遺跡最大級の18号窯（530年頃）を覆屋で保護し、出土した埴輪3点も展示。 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|遠景 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|1号・2号工房（復元） File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|工房と埴輪窯跡（植え込み部分） ファイル:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|出土品
{{small|[[今城塚古代歴史館]]展示。}} == 利用情報 == * 開館時間：10時 - 17時（公園内は終日オープン） * 休館日：12月29日 - 1月3日 * 入館料：無料 == 交通アクセス == * [[JR京都線]][[摂津富田駅]]から高槻市営バス阿武山団地、日赤病院行、上土室下車東へ徒歩約5分。 == 周辺 == * [[上の池公園]]（隣接） * 番山古墳 * [[今城塚古墳]] * [[今城塚古代歴史館]] * [[太田茶臼山古墳]] == 出典 == == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1805|今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html ハニワ工場公園] - 高槻市ホームページ「インターネット歴史館」 {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しせきしんいけはにわこうはこうえん}} [[Category:大阪府の公園]] [[Category:大阪府にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:高槻市の歴史]] [[Category:高槻市の地理]] [[Category:1995年開業の施設]]

{{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|51|14.1|N|135|35|4.6|E|region:JP-27}} |分類 = 史跡公園 |面積 = 4,163m2 |前身 = |開園 = [[1995年]](平成7年)3月 |運営者 = 高槻市 |年来園者数 = |現況 = |設備・遊具 = |駐車場 = なし |バリアフリー = |建築家と技術者 = |告示 = |事務所 = |事務所所在地 = |アクセス = |備考 = |map=Japan Osaka |公式サイト = [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園] }} '''史跡新池ハニワ工場公園'''（しせきしんいけハニワこうばこうえん）は、[[大阪府]][[高槻市]]にある[[史跡]][[公園]]。'''新池埴輪製作遺跡'''（国の[[史跡]]「今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡」の一部）を保存する公園である。 == 概要 == 新池埴輪製作遺跡は、5 - 6世紀の遺跡で、埴輪窯18基と工房、工人集落からなる全国最大級の[[埴輪]]工場跡である。一帯は『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年条にある「摂津国三嶋郡植廬（ハニイホ）新羅人之先祖也」と考えられている。[[今城塚古墳]]（高槻市郡家新町、6世紀前半）の埴輪は当工場で造られた高槻市教育委員会文化財課「ハニワ工場公園」リーフレット。 遺跡域では[[1988年]]（[[昭和]]63年）から調査がはじまり、[[1994年]]（[[平成]]6年）に「ハニワ工場公園」として整備された。[[1991年]]（平成3年）7月20日、遺跡域が既指定の国の[[史跡]]「今城塚古墳」に追加指定され、史跡指定名称が「今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡」に変更。 == ハニワ工場の略年 == * 450年頃、太田茶臼山古墳の造営を目的として工場が開かれた。窯3基（A群）と工房3棟、住居7棟の規模。 * 480年頃、窯5基（B群）と住居7棟を追加。番山古墳などのハニワを生産。 * 530年頃、窯10基（C群）を追加。5棟の工房（住居兼用）、住居14棟。今城塚古墳向けのハニワを生産。 * 550年頃、ハニワ生産を終える。 == 施設 == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|ハニワ工場館（新池遺跡18号窯）]] * プロムナード ** 動物、円筒、家形、武人埴輪などの複製品を20種類、21体設置。本物と同寸法。磁器製。素焼きの質感を模しており自由に触れる継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.157(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN 4-642-07735-9)。 ** ヨシトミヤスオの漫画による説明パネルを展示（陶板製）。 * 工房2棟 ** 3棟の内2棟を復元。[[竪穴建物]]で、母屋にあたる部分は茅葺き、周縁の下屋部分は杉皮葺きで復元されている継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.158(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN 4-642-07735-9)。 * ハニワ窯跡2基 ** 大小あわせて18の窯の内、2基を復元。また、1基を発掘中の状態で再現。 * ハニワ工場館 ** 新池遺跡最大級の18号窯（530年頃）を覆屋で保護し、出土した埴輪3点も展示。 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|遠景 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|1号・2号工房（復元） File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|工房と埴輪窯跡（植え込み部分） ファイル:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|出土品
{{small|[[今城塚古代歴史館]]展示。}} == 利用情報 == * 開館時間：10時 - 17時（公園内は終日オープン） * 休館日：12月29日 - 1月3日 * 入館料：無料 == 交通アクセス == * [[JR京都線]][[摂津富田駅]]から高槻市営バス阿武山団地、日赤病院行、上土室下車東へ徒歩約5分。 == 周辺 == * [[上の池公園]]（隣接） * 番山古墳 * [[今城塚古墳]] * [[今城塚古代歴史館]] * [[太田茶臼山古墳]] == 出典 == == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1805|今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html ハニワ工場公園] - 高槻市ホームページ「インターネット歴史館」 {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しせきしんいけはにわこうはこうえん}} [[Category:大阪府にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:高槻市の歴史]] [[Category:高槻市の公園]] [[Category:1995年開業の施設]]

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the..."
revision.text:
'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * **Promenade** - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * **Workshops** - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * **Haniwa kiln remains** - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * **Haniwa Factory Hall** - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}}.}} == Visitor Information == * **Opening hours**: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * **Closed**: December 29 – January 3 * **Admission**: Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]]

'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * **Promenade** - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * **Workshops** - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * **Haniwa kiln remains** - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * **Haniwa Factory Hall** - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. {{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}}.}} == Visitor Information == * **Opening hours**: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * **Closed**: December 29 – January 3 * **Admission**: Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[category:new pages]][[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]]

'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * **Promenade** - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * **Workshops** - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * **Haniwa kiln remains** - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * **Haniwa Factory Hall** - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * **Promenade** - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * **Workshops** - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * **Haniwa kiln remains** - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * **Haniwa Factory Hall** - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|Artifacts

Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]]

revision.text:
'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * **Promenade** - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * **Workshops** - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * **Haniwa kiln remains** - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * **Haniwa Factory Hall** - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}}.}} == Visitor Information == * **Opening hours**: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * **Closed**: December 29 – January 3 * **Admission**: Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]]

'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * **Promenade** - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * **Workshops** - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * **Haniwa kiln remains** - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * **Haniwa Factory Hall** - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}}.}} == Visitor Information == * **Opening hours**: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * **Closed**: December 29 – January 3 * **Admission**: Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]]

'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}} * {{ill|Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]]

The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}} * {{ill|Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]]

It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. revision.text:
'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}} * {{ill|Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]]

'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}} * {{ill|Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. revision.text:
'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}} * {{ill|Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Shinike Haniwa Production Site}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinike Haniwa Production Site}}

The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. revision.text:
'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}} * {{ill|Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' - Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). - Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' - Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' - Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' - Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館}} * {{ill|Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm
