On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=draft|12=draft}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅|12=draft|12=draft}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|12=draft|12=draft}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[en:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinike Haniwa Production Site}}

'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|ja|摂津富田駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[en:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinike Haniwa Production Site}}

revision.comment: 30 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園 up to rev 99869601
revision.text:
{{公園 |名称 = 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園 |英語名称 = |画像 = [[File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-1.jpg|300px]] |画像キャプション = （手前から）1号・2号・3号埴輪窯
（1号・2号は復元） |国 = {{JPN}} |都市 = [[大阪府]][[高槻市]]上土室1丁目
{{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|51|14.1|N|135|35|4.6|E|region:JP-27}} |分類 = 史跡公園 |面積 = 4,163m2 |前身 = |開園 = [[1995年]](平成7年)3月 |運営者 = 高槻市 |年来園者数 = |現況 = |設備・遊具 = |駐車場 = なし |バリアフリー = |建築家と技術者 = |告示 = |事務所 = |事務所所在地 = |アクセス = |備考 = |map=Japan Osaka |公式サイト = [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html 史跡新池ハニワ工場公園] }} '''史跡新池ハニワ工場公園'''（しせきしんいけハニワこうばこうえん）は、[[大阪府]][[高槻市]]にある[[史跡]][[公園]]。'''新池埴輪製作遺跡'''（国の[[史跡]]「今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡」の一部）を保存する公園である。 == 概要 == 新池埴輪製作遺跡は、5 - 6世紀の遺跡で、埴輪窯18基と工房、工人集落からなる全国最大級の[[埴輪]]工場跡である。一帯は『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年条にある「摂津国三嶋郡植廬（ハニイホ）新羅人之先祖也」と考えられている。[[今城塚古墳]]（高槻市郡家新町、6世紀前半）の埴輪は当工場で造られた高槻市教育委員会文化財課「ハニワ工場公園」リーフレット。 遺跡域では[[1988年]]（[[昭和]]63年）から調査がはじまり、[[1994年]]（[[平成]]6年）に「ハニワ工場公園」として整備された。[[1991年]]（平成3年）7月20日、遺跡域が既指定の国の[[史跡]]「今城塚古墳」に追加指定され、史跡指定名称が「今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡」に変更。 == ハニワ工場の略年 == * 450年頃、太田茶臼山古墳の造営を目的として工場が開かれた。窯3基（A群）と工房3棟、住居7棟の規模。 * 480年頃、窯5基（B群）と住居7棟を追加。番山古墳などのハニワを生産。 * 530年頃、窯10基（C群）を追加。5棟の工房（住居兼用）、住居14棟。今城塚古墳向けのハニワを生産。 * 550年頃、ハニワ生産を終える。 == 施設 == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|ハニワ工場館（新池遺跡18号窯）]] * プロムナード ** 動物、円筒、家形、武人埴輪などの複製品を20種類、21体設置。本物と同寸法。磁器製。素焼きの質感を模しており自由に触れる継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.157(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN 4-642-07735-9)。 ** ヨシトミヤスオの漫画による説明パネルを展示（陶板製）。 * 工房2棟 ** 3棟の内2棟を復元。[[竪穴建物]]で、母屋にあたる部分は茅葺き、周縁の下屋部分は杉皮葺きで復元されている継体天皇と今城塚古墳 P.158(高槻市教育委員会編 ISBN 4-642-07735-9)。 * ハニワ窯跡2基 ** 大小あわせて18の窯の内、2基を復元。また、1基を発掘中の状態で再現。 * ハニワ工場館 ** 新池遺跡最大級の18号窯（530年頃）を覆屋で保護し、出土した埴輪3点も展示。 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|遠景 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|1号・2号工房（復元） File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|工房と埴輪窯跡（植え込み部分） ファイル:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|出土品
{{small|[[今城塚古代歴史館]]展示。}} == 利用情報 == * 開館時間：10時 - 17時（公園内は終日オープン） * 休館日：12月29日 - 1月3日 * 入館料：無料 == 交通アクセス == * [[JR京都線]][[摂津富田駅]]から高槻市営バス公団阿武山・日赤病院行きに乗車、上土室で下車し東へ徒歩約5分。 == 周辺 == * [[上の池公園]]（隣接） * 番山古墳 * [[今城塚古墳]] * [[今城塚古代歴史館]] * [[太田茶臼山古墳]] == 出典 == == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1805|今城塚古墳 附 新池埴輪製作遺跡}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html ハニワ工場公園] - 高槻市ホームページ「インターネット歴史館」 {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しせきしんいけはにわこうはこうえん}} [[Category:大阪府にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:高槻市の歴史]] [[Category:高槻市の公園]] [[Category:1995年開業の施設]]

'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|ja|新池埴輪製作遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. revision.text:
'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in [[:en:Takatsuki|Takatsuki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. The park preserves the [[:en:Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site]], which is part of the nationally designated historic site [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|qq=}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] [[:en:Settsu-Tonda Station|Settsu-Tonda Station]], take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uenoike Park|qq=}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banzan Kofun|qq=}} * [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|史跡新池ハニワ工場公園|version=99869601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[en:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] [[Category:1995年開業の施設]]

revision.text:
'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in [[:en:Takatsuki|Takatsuki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. The park preserves the [[:en:Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site]], which is part of the nationally designated historic site [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|qq=}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] [[:en:Settsu-Tonda Station|Settsu-Tonda Station]], take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uenoike Park|qq=}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banzan Kofun|qq=}} * [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|史跡新池ハニワ工場公園|version=99869601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[en:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] [[Category:1995年開業の施設]] [[Category:National historic sites in Osaka prefecture]] [[Category:高槻市の公園]] [[Category:高槻市の歴史]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in [[:en:Takatsuki|Takatsuki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. The park preserves the [[:en:Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site]], which is part of the nationally designated historic site [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[:en:1988|1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|qq=}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[JR Kyoto Line]] [[:en:Settsu-Tonda Station|Settsu-Tonda Station]], take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uenoike Park|qq=}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banzan Kofun|qq=}} * [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|史跡新池ハニワ工場公園|version=99869601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Sites of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[Category:1995年開業の施設]] [[Category:National historic sites in Osaka prefecture]] [[Category:高槻市の公園]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]]

The haniwa for the [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[:en:1988|1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[:en:1994|1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods

Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uenoike Park|qq=}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banzan Kofun|qq=}} * [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|史跡新池ハニワ工場公園|version=99869601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1995]] [[Category:National historic sites in Osaka prefecture]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11412131]] Wikidata name: Shinchi Haniwa Production Site Park [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in [[:en:Takatsuki|Takatsuki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. The park preserves the [[:en:Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site]], which is part of the nationally designated historic site [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[:en:1988|1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[:en:1994|1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods

Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uenoike Park|qq=|1=Uenoike Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26471436}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banzan Kofun|qq=}} * [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|qq=|1=Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376793}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|史跡新池ハニワ工場公園|version=99869601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Shinike Haniwa Production Site]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1995]] [[Category:National historic sites in Osaka prefecture]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]]

[[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412131]] Wikidata name: Shinchi Haniwa Production Site Park [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in [[:en:Takatsuki|Takatsuki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. The park preserves the [[:en:Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site]], which is part of the nationally designated historic site [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[:en:1988|1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[:en:1994|1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods

revision.text:
[[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412131]] Wikidata name: Shinchi Haniwa Production Site Park [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in [[:en:Takatsuki|Takatsuki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. The park preserves the [[:en:Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site]], which is part of the nationally designated historic site [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[:en:1988|1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[:en:1994|1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|qq=}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[:en:JR Kyoto Line|JR Kyoto Line]] [[:en:Settsu-Tonda Station|Settsu-Tonda Station]], take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uenoike Park|1=Uenoike Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26471436}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banzan Kofun|qq=}} * [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|1=Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376793}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|史跡新池ハニワ工場公園|version=99869601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1995]] [[Category:National historic sites in Osaka prefecture]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] {{wikidata link|Q11412131}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uenoike Park|1=Uenoike Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26471436}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banzan Kofun|qq=}} * [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|1=Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376793}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|史跡新池ハニワ工場公園|version=99869601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1995]] [[Category:National historic sites in Osaka prefecture]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] {{wikidata link|Q11412131}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11412131}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412131]] Wikidata name: Shinchi Haniwa Production Site Park [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in [[:en:Takatsuki|Takatsuki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. The park preserves the [[:en:Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site]], which is part of the nationally designated historic site [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[:en:1988|1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[:en:1994|1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods

[[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412131]] Wikidata name: Shinchi Haniwa Production Site Park [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in [[:en:Takatsuki|Takatsuki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. The park preserves the [[:en:Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site]], which is part of the nationally designated historic site [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[:en:1988|1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[:en:1994|1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|qq=}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[:en:JR Kyoto Line|JR Kyoto Line]] [[:en:Settsu-Tonda Station|Settsu-Tonda Station]], take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uenoike Park|1=Uenoike Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26471436}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banzan Kofun|qq=}} * [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|1=Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376793}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uenoike Park|1=Uenoike Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26471436}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banzan Kofun|qq=}} * [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|1=Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376793}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|史跡新池ハニワ工場公園|version=99869601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1995]] [[Category:National historic sites in Osaka prefecture]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11412131}}

[[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412131]] Wikidata name: Shinchi Haniwa Production Site Park [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in [[:en:Takatsuki|Takatsuki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. The park preserves the [[:en:Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site]], which is part of the nationally designated historic site [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[:en:1988|1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[:en:1994|1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods

Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uenoike Park|1=Uenoike Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26471436}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banzan Kofun|qq=}} * [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|1=Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376793}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|史跡新池ハニワ工場公園|version=99869601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1995]] [[Category:National historic sites in Osaka prefecture]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11412131}}

revision.text:
[[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412131]] Wikidata name: Shinchi Haniwa Production Site Park [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in [[:en:Takatsuki|Takatsuki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. The park preserves the [[:en:Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site]], which is part of the nationally designated historic site [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[:en:1988|1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[:en:1994|1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Displayed at {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|qq=}}.}} == Visitor Information == * '''Opening hours''': 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (park area open all day) * '''Closed''': December 29 – January 3 * '''Admission''': Free == Access == * From [[:en:JR Kyoto Line|JR Kyoto Line]] [[:en:Settsu-Tonda Station|Settsu-Tonda Station]], take Takatsuki City Bus bound for Kōdan Abuyama or Nippon Red Cross Hospital. Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uenoike Park|1=Uenoike Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26471436}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banzan Kofun|qq=}} * [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|1=Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376793}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|史跡新池ハニワ工場公園|version=99869601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] {{wikidata link|Q11412131}} [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1995]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:National historic sites in Osaka prefecture]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. revision.text:
[[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412131]] Wikidata name: Shinchi Haniwa Production Site Park [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in [[:en:Takatsuki|Takatsuki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. The park preserves the [[:en:Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site]], which is part of the nationally designated historic site [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the [[:en:Imashirozuka Kofun|Imashirozuka Kofun]] (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in [[:en:1988|1988]] (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in [[:en:1994|1994]] (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods

Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts.
