Get off at Kamitsuchimuro and walk about 5 minutes east. == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Uenoike Park|ja|上の池公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uenoike Park|1=Uenoike Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26471436}} (adjacent) * {{ill|Banzan Kofun|ja|番山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banzan Kofun|qq=}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|en|Imashirozuka Kofun|WD=Q11376795|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun}} * {{ill|Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imajōzuka Ancient History Museum|1=Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376793}} * {{ill|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|lt=Ota Chausuyama Kofun|ja|太田茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat|Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} {{osm box|w|156761606}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1805|Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}} * [http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi_kanko/rekishi/rekishikan/shisetsu/1327317816926.html Haniwa Factory Park] – Takatsuki City Internet History Museum {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|史跡新池ハニワ工場公園|version=99869601|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] {{wikidata link|Q11412131}} [[Category:1995 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1995]] [[Category:History of Takatsuki]] [[Category:National historic sites in Osaka prefecture]] [[Category:Parks in Takatsuki]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412131]] Wikidata name: Shinchi Haniwa Production Site Park [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|en|Takatsuki|WD=Q335423|lt=Takatsuki}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|en|Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|WD=Q133281927|lt=Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|en|Imashirozuka Kofun|WD=Q11376795|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|en|Imashirozuka Kofun|WD=Q11376795|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in {{ill|1988|en|1988|WD=Q2426|lt=1988}} (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in {{ill|1994|en|1994|WD=Q2067|lt=1994}} (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts. File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site, gaikan.jpg|Distant view File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-2.jpg|Reconstructed workshops 1 and 2 File:Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site-3.jpg|Workshops and haniwa kiln remains (planted area) File:新池埴輪製作遺跡出土品.JPG|burial goods

[[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] [[ja:史跡新池ハニワ工場公園]] '''Shiseki Shin-ike Haniwa Kōbā Kōen''' (史跡新池ハニワ工場公園) is a historical park located in {{ill|Takatsuki|en|Takatsuki|WD=Q335423|lt=Takatsuki|1=Takatsuki}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, Japan. The park preserves the {{ill|Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|en|Shinchi Haniwa Production Site|WD=Q133281927|lt=Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site|1=Shinchi Haniwa Production Site}}, which is part of the nationally designated historic site {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|en|Imashirozuka Kofun|WD=Q11376795|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|1=Imashirozuka Kofun}} and its attached facilities. == Overview == The Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site dates to the 5th and 6th centuries and comprises 18 haniwa kilns, workshops, and worker settlements, making it one of the largest haniwa production sites in Japan. It is believed to correspond to the "Settsu Province Mishima District's Uetahaniho, home to the ancestors of Silla people" mentioned in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in the 23rd year of Emperor Kinmei. The haniwa for the {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|en|Imashirozuka Kofun|WD=Q11376795|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|1=Imashirozuka Kofun}} (mid-6th century) were produced at this site.Takatsuki City Board of Education Cultural Properties Division, "Haniwa Factory Park" leaflet. Excavations began in {{ill|1988|en|1988|WD=Q2426|lt=1988|1=1988}} (Shōwa 63), and the site was developed into "Haniwa Factory Park" in {{ill|1994|en|1994|WD=Q2067|lt=1994|1=1994}} (Heisei 6). On July 20, 1991, the site was added to the national historic site designation of "Imajōzuka Kofun," which was renamed to "Imajōzuka Kofun with Shin-ike Haniwa Production Site." == Chronology of the Haniwa Factory == * Around 450 AD: Established to produce haniwa for the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun. Initial setup included 3 kilns (Group A), 3 workshops, and 7 residential buildings. * Around 480 AD: Expanded with 5 additional kilns (Group B) and 7 more residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Banzan Kofun and others. * Around 530 AD: Further expanded with 10 kilns (Group C), 5 dual-use workshop-residences, and 14 residential buildings. Produced haniwa for the Imajōzuka Kofun. * Around 550 AD: Haniwa production ceased. == Facilities == [[File:Haniwa-park 02.jpg|thumb|Haniwa Factory Hall (18th kiln of the Shin-ike site)]] * '''Promenade''' ** Features replicas of various types of haniwa, including animals, cylinders, houses, and warriors. Twenty-one replicas are installed, all life-sized and made of ceramic to mimic terracotta texture. Visitors are free to touch them."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 157, Takatsuki City Board of Education (ISBN 4-642-07735-9). ** Panels with explanations in manga style by Yoshitomi Yasuo are displayed (made of ceramic tiles). * '''Workshops''' ** Two of the original three pit-dwelling workshops have been reconstructed. The main areas are thatched, while the peripheral sections are roofed with cedar bark."Emperor Keitai and Imajōzuka Kofun," p. 158. * '''Haniwa kiln remains''' ** Of the 18 kilns discovered, two have been reconstructed, and one is displayed in an excavation state. * '''Haniwa Factory Hall''' ** Protects the largest kiln (18th kiln, ca. 530 AD) with a shelter and exhibits three excavated haniwa artifacts.
